{{short description|Type of functional magnetic resonance imaging}}
{{About|resting state networks and functional imaging|information regarding the Default Mode Network specifically|Default mode network|other uses|Rest (disambiguation)}}
{{Infobox diagnostic
| name            = Resting state fMRI
| image           = File:Temporal-Non-Local-Means-Filtering-Reveals-Real-Time-Whole-Brain-Cortical-Interactions-in-Resting-pone.0158504.s002.ogv|
| alt             =
| caption         = Movie of the [[in vivo]] BOLD signal from the [[Cortex (anatomy)|cortical surface]] of a human subject from [[Human Connectome Project|HCP]], acquired using resting state fMRI, pre-processed to suppress the noise in data<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Smith SM, Beckmann CF, Andersson J, Auerbach EJ, Bijsterbosch J, Douaud G, Duff E, Feinberg DA, Griffanti L, Harms MP, Kelly M, Laumann T, Miller KL, Moeller S, Petersen S, Power J, Salimi-Khorshidi G, Snyder AZ, Vu AT, Woolrich MW, Xu J, Yacoub E, Uğurbil K, Van Essen DC, Glasser MF | display-authors = 6 | title = Resting-state fMRI in the Human Connectome Project | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 80 | pages = 144–168 | date = October 2013 | pmid = 23702415 | pmc = 3720828 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2013.05.039 }}</ref><ref name="CBhushanPlosOne">{{cite journal | vauthors = Bhushan C, Chong M, Choi S, Joshi AA, Haldar JP, Damasio H, Leahy RM | title = Temporal Non-Local Means Filtering Reveals Real-Time Whole-Brain Cortical Interactions in Resting fMRI | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 11 | issue = 7 | article-number = e0158504 | date = 2016-07-08 | pmid = 27391481 | pmc = 4938391 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0158504 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2016PLoSO..1158504B }}</ref> and played back at a real-time rate. The BOLD signal intensities are visualized on a smoothed cortical surface. At each point on the [[Cortex (anatomy)|cortex]], white color represents the average BOLD signal, while blue and red colors represents lower and higher signal than average BOLD signal respectively.<ref name="CBhushanPlosOne" />
| pronounce       = 
| purpose         = Evaluate regional interactions that occur in resting state(brain mapping)

| LOINC           = <!--{{LOINC|code}}-->
}}

'''Resting state fMRI''' ('''rs-fMRI''' or '''R-fMRI'''), also referred to as '''task-independent fMRI''' or '''task-free fMRI''', is a method of [[functional magnetic resonance imaging]] (fMRI) that is used in [[brain mapping]] to evaluate regional interactions that occur in a resting or task-negative state, when an explicit task is not being performed.<ref name="Biswal1">{{cite journal | vauthors = Biswal BB | title = Resting state fMRI: a personal history | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 62 | issue = 2 | pages = 938–944 | date = August 2012 | pmid = 22326802 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2012.01.090 | s2cid = 93823 }}</ref><ref name="Buckner">{{cite journal | vauthors = Buckner RL, Krienen FM, Yeo BT | title = Opportunities and limitations of intrinsic functional connectivity MRI | journal = Nature Neuroscience | volume = 16 | issue = 7 | pages = 832–837 | date = July 2013 | pmid = 23799476 | doi = 10.1038/nn.3423 | s2cid = 17141252 }}</ref> A number of resting-state brain networks have been identified, one of which is the [[default mode network]].<ref name="Sharaev">{{cite journal | vauthors = Sharaev MG, Zavyalova VV, Ushakov VL, Kartashov SI, Velichkovsky BM | title = Effective Connectivity within the Default Mode Network: Dynamic Causal Modeling of Resting-State fMRI Data | journal = Frontiers in Human Neuroscience | volume = 10 | page = 14 | date = 2016 | pmid = 26869900 | pmc = 4740785 | doi = 10.3389/fnhum.2016.00014 | doi-access = free }}</ref> These brain networks are observed  through changes in [[Cerebral blood flow|blood flow in the brain]] which creates what is referred to as a [[blood-oxygen-level dependent]] (BOLD) signal that can be measured using fMRI.

Because [[brain function|brain activity]] is intrinsic, present even in the absence of an externally prompted task, any brain region will have spontaneous fluctuations in BOLD signal. The resting state approach is useful to explore the brain's functional organization and to examine if it is altered in [[Neurological disorder|neurological]] or [[mental disorder]]s. Because of the resting state aspect of this imaging, data can be collected from a range of patient groups including people with intellectual disabilities, pediatric groups, and even those that are unconscious.<ref name="Agcaoglu_2019">{{cite journal | vauthors = Agcaoglu O, Wilson TW, Wang YP, Stephen J, Calhoun VD | title = Resting state connectivity differences in eyes open versus eyes closed conditions | journal = Human Brain Mapping | volume = 40 | issue = 8 | pages = 2488–2498 | date = June 2019 | pmid = 30720907 | pmc = 6865559 | doi = 10.1002/hbm.24539 }}</ref><ref name="Smitha_2017">{{cite journal | vauthors = Smitha KA, Akhil Raja K, Arun KM, Rajesh PG, Thomas B, Kapilamoorthy TR, Kesavadas C | title = Resting state fMRI: A review on methods in resting state connectivity analysis and resting state networks | journal = The Neuroradiology Journal | volume = 30 | issue = 4 | pages = 305–317 | date = August 2017 | pmid = 28353416 | pmc = 5524274 | doi = 10.1177/1971400917697342 }}</ref> Resting-state functional connectivity research has revealed a number of networks which are consistently found in healthy subjects, different stages of consciousness and across species, and represent specific patterns of synchronous activity.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Biswal BB | year = 2011 | title = Resting State Functional Connectivity | doi = 10.1016/j.biopsych.2011.03.032 | journal = Biological Psychiatry | volume = 69 | issue = 9| pages = 200S | s2cid = 142478873 }}</ref><ref name="Rosazza">{{cite journal | vauthors = Rosazza C, Minati L | title = Resting-state brain networks: literature review and clinical applications | journal = Neurological Sciences | volume = 32 | issue = 5 | pages = 773–785 | date = October 2011 | pmid = 21667095 | doi = 10.1007/s10072-011-0636-y | s2cid = 17222 }}</ref><ref name="Cole">{{cite journal | vauthors = Cole DM, Smith SM, Beckmann CF | title = Advances and pitfalls in the analysis and interpretation of resting-state FMRI data | journal = Frontiers in Systems Neuroscience | volume = 4 | page = 8 | date = 2010 | pmid = 20407579 | pmc = 2854531 | doi = 10.3389/fnsys.2010.00008 | doi-access = free }}</ref>
<!--The concept of resting state fMRI has been present for nearly two decades, yet skepticism regarding the actual source of the neurological signal prevented it from becoming a common method of imaging until recently, where publications studying various aspects of the brain using resting state fMRI has increased nearly 600 articles in peer-reviewed literature for 2012 alone.-->

==Basics of resting state fMRI==
[[File:Haxby2001.jpg|right|thumb|400px|alt=fMRI images from a study showing parts of the brain lighting up on seeing houses and other parts on seeing faces|These fMRI images are from a study showing parts of the brain lighting up on seeing houses and other parts on seeing faces. The 'r' values are correlations, with higher positive or negative values indicating a better match.]]

Functional magnetic resonance imaging (functional MRI or fMRI) is a specific [[magnetic resonance imaging]] (MRI) procedure that measures brain activity by detecting associated changes in blood flow. More specifically, brain activity is measured through low frequency BOLD signal in the brain.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = DeYoe EA, Bandettini P, Neitz J, Miller D, Winans P | title = Functional magnetic resonance imaging (FMRI) of the human brain | journal = Journal of Neuroscience Methods | volume = 54 | issue = 2 | pages = 171–187 | date = October 1994 | pmid = 7869750 | doi = 10.1016/0165-0270(94)90191-0 | s2cid = 3718293 }}</ref>

The procedure is similar to MRI but uses the change in magnetization between oxygen-rich and oxygen-poor blood as its basic measure. This measure is frequently corrupted by noise from various sources and hence statistical procedures are used to extract the underlying signal. The resulting brain activation can be presented graphically by color-coding the strength of activation across the brain or the specific region studied. The technique can localize activity to within millimeters but, using standard techniques, no better than within a window of a few seconds.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bandettini PA | title = Seven topics in functional magnetic resonance imaging | journal = Journal of Integrative Neuroscience | volume = 8 | issue = 3 | pages = 371–403 | date = September 2009 | pmid = 19938211 | pmc = 3143579 | doi = 10.1142/s0219635209002186 }}</ref>

FMRI is used both in research, and to a lesser extent, in clinical settings. It can also be combined and complemented with other measures of brain physiology such as [[EEG]],  [[Near infrared spectroscopy|NIRS]], and functional ultrasound.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bandettini P | title = Functional MRI today | journal = International Journal of Psychophysiology | volume = 63 | issue = 2 | pages = 138–145 | date = February 2007 | pmid = 16842871 | doi = 10.1016/j.ijpsycho.2006.03.016 }}</ref><ref name="Korhonen2014">{{cite journal | vauthors = Korhonen V, Hiltunen T, Myllylä T, Wang X, Kantola J, Nikkinen J, Zang YF, LeVan P, Kiviniemi V | display-authors = 6 | title = Synchronous multiscale neuroimaging environment for critically sampled physiological analysis of brain function: hepta-scan concept | journal = Brain Connectivity | volume = 4 | issue = 9 | pages = 677–689 | date = November 2014 | pmid = 25131996 | pmc = 4238249 | doi = 10.1089/brain.2014.0258 }}</ref> [[Arterial spin labeling]] fMRI can be used as a complementary approach for assessing resting brain functions.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Chuang KH, van Gelderen P, Merkle H, Bodurka J, Ikonomidou VN, Koretsky AP, Duyn JH, Talagala SL | display-authors = 6 | title = Mapping resting-state functional connectivity using perfusion MRI | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 40 | issue = 4 | pages = 1595–1605 | date = May 2008 | pmid = 18314354 | pmc = 2435272 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2008.01.006 }}</ref>
<ref>
{{Cite journal |last=Bertolo |first=Adrien  |title=High sensitivity mapping of brain-wide functional networks in awake mice using simultaneous multi-slice fUS imaging |journal=  Imaging Neuroscience|year=2023|volume=1 |pages=1–18 |article-number=imag-1-00030 |doi=10.1162/imag_a_00030 |doi-access=free |pmid=40799722 |pmc=12007538 }}</ref>

==Physiological basis==

The physiological blood-flow response largely decides the temporal sensitivity, how well neurons that are active can be measured in BOLD fMRI. The basic time resolution parameter is the [[sampling rate]], or TR, which dictates how often a particular brain slice is excited and allowed to lose its magnetization. TRs could vary from the very short (500 ms) to the very long (3 seconds). For fMRI specifically, the [[haemodynamic response]] is assumed to last over 10 seconds, rising multiplicatively (that is, as a proportion of current value), peaking at 4 to 6 seconds, and then falling multiplicatively. Changes in the blood-flow system, the vascular system, integrate responses to neuronal activity over time. Because this response is a smooth continuous function, sampling with faster TRs helps only to map faster fluctuations like respiratory and heart rate signals.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Huettel SA, Song AW, McCarthy G |title=Functional magnetic resonance imaging |date=2008 |publisher=Sinauer Associates |location=Sunderland, Mass. |isbn=978-0-87893-286-3 |edition=2nd}}</ref>

While fMRI strives to measure the neuronal activity in the brain, the BOLD signal can be influenced by many other physiological factors other than neuronal activity. For example, respiratory fluctuations and cardiovascular cycles affect the BOLD signal being measured in the brain and therefore are usually tried to be removed during processing of the raw fMRI data. Due to these sources of noise, there have been many experts who have approached the idea of resting state fMRI very skeptically during the early uses of fMRI. It has only been very recently that researchers have become confident that the signal being measured is not an artifact caused by other physiological function.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Damoiseaux JS, Rombouts SA, Barkhof F, Scheltens P, Stam CJ, Smith SM, Beckmann CF | title = Consistent resting-state networks across healthy subjects | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 103 | issue = 37 | pages = 13848–13853 | date = September 2006 | pmid = 16945915 | pmc = 1564249 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.0601417103 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2006PNAS..10313848D }}</ref>

Resting state [[Dynamic functional connectivity|functional connectivity]] between spatially distinct brain regions reflects the repeated history of co-activation patterns within these regions, thereby serving as a measure of [[Neuroplasticity|plasticity]].<ref name="Guerra">{{cite journal | vauthors = Guerra-Carrillo B, Mackey AP, Bunge SA | title = Resting-state fMRI: a window into human brain plasticity | journal = The Neuroscientist | volume = 20 | issue = 5 | pages = 522–533 | date = October 2014 | pmid = 24561514 | doi = 10.1177/1073858414524442 | s2cid = 13300284 }}</ref>

==History==
'''Bharat Biswal'''

In 1992, [https://biomedical.njit.edu/faculty/biswal Bharat Biswal] started his work as a graduate student at The [[Medical College of Wisconsin]] under the direction of his advisor, [[James S. Hyde]], and discovered that the brain, even during rest, contains information about its functional organization. He had used fMRI to study how different regions of the brain communicate while the brain is at rest and not performing any active task. Though at the time, Biswal's research was mostly disregarded and attributed to another signal source, his resting neuroimaging technique has now been widely replicated and considered a valid method of [[Brain mapping|mapping]] functional brain networks. Mapping the brain's activity while it is at rest holds many potentials for brain research and even helps doctors diagnose various diseases of the brain.<ref name="Biswal1" />

'''Marcus Raichle'''

Experiments by neurologist [[Marcus Raichle]]'s lab at [[Washington University School of Medicine]] and other groups showed that the brain's energy consumption is increased by less than 5% of its baseline energy consumption while performing a focused mental task. These experiments showed that the brain is constantly active with a high level of activity even when the person is not engaged in focused mental work (the resting state). His lab has been primarily focused on finding the basis of this resting activity and is credited with many groundbreaking discoveries. These include the relative independence of blood flow and oxygen consumption during changes in brain activity, which provided the physiological basis of fMRI, as well the discovery of the well known [[Default network|Default Mode Network]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fox MD, Snyder AZ, Vincent JL, Corbetta M, Van Essen DC, Raichle ME | title = The human brain is intrinsically organized into dynamic, anticorrelated functional networks | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 102 | issue = 27 | pages = 9673–9678 | date = July 2005 | pmid = 15976020 | pmc = 1157105 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.0504136102 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2005PNAS..102.9673F }}</ref>

==Connectivity==

===Functional===

Functional connectivity is the connectivity between brain regions that share functional properties. More specifically, it can be defined as the temporal correlation between spatially remote neurophysiological events, expressed as deviation from statistical independence across these events in distributed neuronal groups and areas.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Biswal BB, Van Kylen J, Hyde JS | title = Simultaneous assessment of flow and BOLD signals in resting-state functional connectivity maps | journal = NMR in Biomedicine | volume = 10 | issue = 4–5 | pages = 165–170 | year = 1997 | pmid = 9430343 | doi = 10.1002/(sici)1099-1492(199706/08)10:4/5<165::aid-nbm454>3.0.co;2-7 | s2cid = 25428304 }}</ref> This applies to both resting state and task-state studies. While functional connectivity can refer to correlations across subjects, runs, blocks, trials, or individual time points, resting state functional connectivity focuses on connectivity assessed across individual BOLD time points during resting conditions.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Friston K | title = Causal modelling and brain connectivity in functional magnetic resonance imaging | journal = PLOS Biology | volume = 7 | issue = 2 | article-number = e33 | date = February 2009 | pmid = 19226186 | pmc = 2642881 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pbio.1000033 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Functional connectivity has also been evaluated using the perfusion time series sampled with arterial spin labeled perfusion fMRI.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fernández-Seara MA, Aznárez-Sanado M, Mengual E, Irigoyen J, Heukamp F, Pastor MA | title = Effects on resting cerebral blood flow and functional connectivity induced by metoclopramide: a perfusion MRI study in healthy volunteers | journal = British Journal of Pharmacology | volume = 163 | issue = 8 | pages = 1639–1652 | date = August 2011 | pmid = 21175574 | pmc = 3166692 | doi = 10.1111/j.1476-5381.2010.01161.x }}</ref> Functional connectivity MRI ({{Not a typo|fcMRI}}), which can include resting state fMRI and task-based MRI, might someday help provide more definitive diagnoses for mental health disorders such as [[bipolar disorder]] and may also aid in understanding the development and progression of [[post-traumatic stress disorder]] as well as evaluate the effect of treatment.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Smith SM | title = The future of FMRI connectivity | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 62 | issue = 2 | pages = 1257–1266 | date = August 2012 | pmid = 22248579 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2012.01.022 | s2cid = 30701163 }}</ref> Functional connectivity has been suggested to be an expression of the network behavior underlying high level cognitive function partially because unlike structural connectivity, functional connectivity often changes on the order of seconds as in the case of [[dynamic functional connectivity]].{{cn|date=November 2023}}

==Networks==
[[File:RestingStateModels.jpg|thumb|Study showing four functional networks that were found to be highly consistent across subjects. These modules include the visual (yellow), sensory/motor (orange) and basal ganglia (red) cortices as well as the default mode network (posterior cingulate, inferior parietal lobes, and medial frontal gyrus; maroon).]]

===Default mode network===
The [[default mode network]] (DMN) is a network of brain regions that are active when an individual is awake and at rest.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Raichle ME | title = The brain's default mode network | journal = Annual Review of Neuroscience | volume = 38 | issue = 1 | pages = 433–447 | date = July 2015 | pmid = 25938726 | doi = 10.1146/annurev-neuro-071013-014030 | doi-access = free }}</ref>  The default mode network is an interconnected and anatomically defined brain system that preferentially activates when individuals focus on internal tasks such as daydreaming, envisioning the future, retrieving memories, and gauging others' perspectives.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Greicius MD, Krasnow B, Reiss AL, Menon V | title = Functional connectivity in the resting brain: a network analysis of the default mode hypothesis | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 100 | issue = 1 | pages = 253–258 | date = January 2003 | pmid = 12506194 | pmc = 140943 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.0135058100 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2003PNAS..100..253G }}</ref> It is negatively correlated with brain systems that focus on external visual signals. It is one of the most studied networks present during resting state and is one of the most easily visualized networks.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Buckner RL | title = The serendipitous discovery of the brain's default network | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 62 | issue = 2 | pages = 1137–1145 | date = August 2012 | pmid = 22037421 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2011.10.035 | s2cid = 9880586 }}</ref>

===Other resting state networks===
Depending on the method of resting state analysis, functional connectivity studies have reported a number of [[Biological neural network|neural networks]] that result to be strongly functionally connected during rest. The key networks, also referred as components, which are more frequently reported include: the DMN, the [[Sensory cortex|sensory]]/[[Motor cortex|motor]] networks, the [[Frontoparietal network|central executive network]] (CEN), up to three different [[Visual cortex|visual]] networks, a [[Salience network|ventral]] and [[Dorsal attention network|dorsal]] attention network, the [[Auditory cortex|auditory]] network and the [[Limbic system|limbic]] network.<ref name="Moussa">{{cite journal | vauthors = Moussa MN, Steen MR, Laurienti PJ, Hayasaka S | title = Consistency of network modules in resting-state FMRI connectome data | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 7 | issue = 8 | article-number = e44428 | year = 2012 | pmid = 22952978 | pmc = 3432126 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0044428 | bibcode = 2012PLoSO...744428M | doi-access = free }}</ref> As already reported, these resting-state networks consist of anatomically separated, but functionally connected regions displaying a high level of correlated BOLD signal activity. These networks are found to be quite consistent across studies, despite differences in the data acquisition and analysis techniques.<ref name="Moussa" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Lee MH, Hacker CD, Snyder AZ, Corbetta M, Zhang D, Leuthardt EC, Shimony JS | title = Clustering of resting state networks | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 7 | issue = 7 | article-number = e40370 | year = 2012 | pmid = 22792291 | pmc = 3392237 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0040370 | bibcode = 2012PLoSO...740370L | doi-access = free }}</ref> Importantly, most of these resting-state components represent known functional networks, that is, regions that are known to share and support cognitive functions.<ref name="Rosazza" />

==Analyzing data==

===Processing data===

Many programs exist for the processing and analyzing of resting state fMRI data. Some of the most commonly used programs include [[Statistical parametric mapping|SPM]], [[Analysis of Functional NeuroImages|AFNI]], [[FMRIB Software Library|FSL]] (esp. Melodic for ICA), [[CONN (functional connectivity toolbox)|CONN]], [https://fcp-indi.github.io/ C-PAC], and Connectome Computation System ([https://github.com/zuoxinian/CCS CCS]).

===Methods of analysis===

There are many methods of both acquiring and processing rsfMRI data. The most popular methods of analysis focus either on independent components or on regions of correlation.{{cn|date=November 2023}}

====Independent component analysis====
[[Independent component analysis]] (ICA) is a useful statistical approach in the detection of resting state networks. ICA separates a signal into non-overlapping spatial and time components.  It is highly data-driven and allows for better removal of noisy components of the signal (motion, scanner drift, etc.). It also has been shown to reliably extract default mode network as well as many other networks with very high consistency.<ref name="Kiviniemi2003">{{cite journal | vauthors = Kiviniemi V, Kantola JH, Jauhiainen J, Hyvärinen A, Tervonen O | title = Independent component analysis of nondeterministic fMRI signal sources | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 19 | issue = 2 Pt 1 | pages = 253–260 | date = June 2003 | pmid = 12814576 | doi = 10.1016/S1053-8119(03)00097-1 | s2cid = 17110486 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Beckmann CF, DeLuca M, Devlin JT, Smith SM | title = Investigations into resting-state connectivity using independent component analysis | journal = Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological Sciences | volume = 360 | issue = 1457 | pages = 1001–1013 | date = May 2005 | pmid = 16087444 | pmc = 1854918 | doi = 10.1098/rstb.2005.1634 }}</ref> ICA remains in the forefront of the research methods.<ref name="Calhoun">{{cite journal | vauthors = Calhoun VD, de Lacy N | title = Ten Key Observations on the Analysis of Resting-state Functional MR Imaging Data Using Independent Component Analysis | journal = Neuroimaging Clinics of North America | volume = 27 | issue = 4 | pages = 561–579 | date = November 2017 | pmid = 28985929 | pmc = 5657522 | doi = 10.1016/j.nic.2017.06.012 }}</ref>

====Regional analysis====
Other methods of observing networks and connectivity in the brain include the [[seed-based d mapping]] and [[region of interest]] (ROI) methods of analysis. In these cases, signal from only a certain [[voxel]] or cluster of voxels known as the seed or ROI are used to calculate correlations with other voxels of the brain. This provides a much more precise and detailed look at specific connectivity in brain areas of interest.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Doyen S, Nicholas P, Poologaindran A, Crawford L, Young IM, Romero-Garcia R, Sughrue ME | title = Connectivity-based parcellation of normal and anatomically distorted human cerebral cortex | journal = Human Brain Mapping | date = November 2021 | volume = 43 | issue = 4 | pages = 1358–1369 | pmid = 34826179 | doi = 10.1002/hbm.25728 | pmc = 8837585 | s2cid = 244660926 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Margulies DS, Kelly AM, Uddin LQ, Biswal BB, Castellanos FX, Milham MP | title = Mapping the functional connectivity of anterior cingulate cortex | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 37 | issue = 2 | pages = 579–588 | date = August 2007 | pmid = 17604651 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2007.05.019 | s2cid = 3330669 | author3-link = Lucina Uddin }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Van Dijk KR, Hedden T, Venkataraman A, Evans KC, Lazar SW, Buckner RL | title = Intrinsic functional connectivity as a tool for human connectomics: theory, properties, and optimization | journal = Journal of Neurophysiology | volume = 103 | issue = 1 | pages = 297–321 | date = January 2010 | pmid = 19889849 | pmc = 2807224 | doi = 10.1152/jn.00783.2009 }}</ref> This can also be performed across the entire brain by utilizing an atlas, making it easier to define ROI's and measure connectivity. In 2021, Yeung and colleagues conducted a regional analysis utilizing a modified version of the Human Connectome Project (HCP) atlas, and found changes in the functional connectome of stroke patients during rehabilitative treatment.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Yeung JT, Young IM, Doyen S, Teo C, Sughrue ME | title = Changes in the Brain Connectome Following Repetitive Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation for Stroke Rehabilitation | journal = Cureus | volume = 13 | issue = 10 | article-number = e19105 | date = October 2021 | pmid = 34858752 | doi = 10.7759/cureus.19105 | doi-access = free | pmc = 8614179 }}</ref> Overall connectivity between an ROI (such as the [[prefrontal cortex]]) and all other voxels of the brain can also be averaged, providing a measure of global brain connectivity (GBC) specific to that ROI.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Cole MW, Yarkoni T, Repovs G, Anticevic A, Braver TS | title = Global connectivity of prefrontal cortex predicts cognitive control and intelligence | journal = The Journal of Neuroscience | volume = 32 | issue = 26 | pages = 8988–8999 | date = June 2012 | pmid = 22745498 | pmc = 3392686 | doi = 10.1523/JNEUROSCI.0536-12.2012 }}</ref>
Other methods for characterizing resting-state networks include partial correlation, coherence and partial coherence, phase relationships, [[dynamic time warping]] distance, clustering, and graph theory.<ref>{{cite journal |author2-link=Gary H. Glover | vauthors = Chang C, Glover GH | title = Time-frequency dynamics of resting-state brain connectivity measured with fMRI | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 50 | issue = 1 | pages = 81–98 | date = March 2010 | pmid = 20006716 | pmc = 2827259 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2009.12.011 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Faria AV, Joel SE, Zhang Y, Oishi K, van Zjil PC, Miller MI, Pekar JJ, Mori S | display-authors = 6 | title = Atlas-based analysis of resting-state functional connectivity: evaluation for reproducibility and multi-modal anatomy-function correlation studies | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 61 | issue = 3 | pages = 613–621 | date = July 2012 | pmid = 22498656 | pmc = 3358461 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2012.03.078 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Meszlényi RJ, Hermann P, Buza K, Gál V, Vidnyánszky Z | title = Resting State fMRI Functional Connectivity Analysis Using Dynamic Time Warping | journal = Frontiers in Neuroscience | volume = 11 | page = 75 | date = 2017-01-01 | pmid = 28261052 | pmc = 5313507 | doi = 10.3389/fnins.2017.00075 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

===Reliability and reproducibility<!-- Dynamics -->===
Resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging (rfMRI) can image low-frequency fluctuations in the spontaneous brain activities, representing a popular tool for macro-scale functional [[connectomics]] to characterize inter-individual differences in normal brain function, mind-brain associations, and the various disorders. This suggests reliability and [[reproducibility]] for commonly used rfMRI-derived measures of the human brain functional [[connectomics]].  These metrics hold great potentials of accelerating biomarker identification for various brain diseases, which call the need of addressing reliability and reproducibility at first place.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Zuo XN, Xing XX | title = Test-retest reliabilities of resting-state FMRI measurements in human brain functional connectomics: a systems neuroscience perspective | journal = Neuroscience and Biobehavioral Reviews | volume = 45 | pages = 100–118 | date = September 2014 | pmid = 24875392 | doi = 10.1016/j.neubiorev.2014.05.009 | s2cid = 20844969 }}</ref>

==Combining imaging techniques==

[[File:Resting State Networks and their topographic EEG signatures.jpg|right|thumb|300px|alt=The left row shows sagittal, coronal and horizontal slices of the ten RSNs (p<0.005; corrected at α<0.05 / x, y & z coordinates are provided at the left bottom corner of each RSN). On the right side the covariance and t-maps for the 8 frequency bands are displayed. A positive covariance value (red) indicates that with increasing RSN activity there is a relative increase in spectral power at a given electrode in a given frequency band, while a negative value (blue) indicates a decrease in power when the RSN activity increases, and vice versa. | This image is from a study using both fMRI and EEG acquisition at the resting state. The left row shows sagittal, coronal and horizontal slices of the ten RSNs. On the right side the covariance and t-maps for the 8 frequency bands are displayed.]]

=== fMRI with DWI ===
With fMRI providing functional and [[Diffusion MRI|DWI]] structural information about the brain, these two imaging techniques are commonly used in conjunction to provide a holistic view of brain network interactions. When collected from defined ROI's, fMRI data informs researchers of how activity (blood flow) in the brain changes over time or during a task.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fox MD, Snyder AZ, Vincent JL, Corbetta M, Van Essen DC, Raichle ME | title = The human brain is intrinsically organized into dynamic, anticorrelated functional networks | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 102 | issue = 27 | pages = 9673–9678 | date = July 2005 | pmid = 15976020 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.0504136102 | pmc = 1157105 | bibcode = 2005PNAS..102.9673F | doi-access = free }}</ref> This is then bolstered through structural DWI data, which shows how individual [[white matter]] tracts connect these ROI's.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Baliyan V, Das CJ, Sharma R, Gupta AK | title = Diffusion weighted imaging: Technique and applications | journal = World Journal of Radiology | volume = 8 | issue = 9 | pages = 785–798 | date = September 2016 | pmid = 27721941 | pmc = 5039674 | doi = 10.4329/wjr.v8.i9.785 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Investigations harnessing these techniques have progressed the field of network neuroscience, by further defining groups of regions in the brain which connect both structurally (having white matter tracts pass between them), and functionally (showing similar or opposite patterns of activity over time), into brain networks like the [[Default mode network|DMN]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Baker CM, Burks JD, Briggs RG, Conner AK, Glenn CA, Sali G, McCoy TM, Battiste JD, O'Donoghue DL, Sughrue ME | display-authors = 6 | title = A Connectomic Atlas of the Human Cerebrum-Chapter 1: Introduction, Methods, and Significance | journal = Operative Neurosurgery | volume = 15 | issue = suppl_1 | pages = S1–S9 | date = December 2018 | pmid = 30260422 | pmc = 6887907 | doi = 10.1093/ons/opy253 }}</ref> Advances in [[topological data analysis]] have established a coherent statistical framework for integrating functional and structural information, represented as functional and structural [[Connectome#As_a_network_or_graph|brain networks]] with distinct topologies.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Songdechakraiwut T, Chung MK | title = Topological learning for brain networks | journal = The Annals of Applied Statistics | volume = 17 | issue = 1 | pages = 403–433 | year = 2023 | publisher = Institute of Mathematical Statistics | doi = 10.1214/22-AOAS1633 | pmc = 9997114 | pmid = 36911168 }}</ref>

This combined data provides unique clinical and neuropsychiatric benefit, by enabling the investigation of how brain networks are disturbed, or white matter pathways compromised, by the presence of mental illness or structural damage.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Dadario NB, Brahimaj B, Yeung J, Sughrue ME | title = Reducing the Cognitive Footprint of Brain Tumor Surgery | journal = Frontiers in Neurology | volume = 12 | article-number = 711646 | date = 2021 | pmid = 34484105 | pmc = 8415405 | doi = 10.3389/fneur.2021.711646 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Altered brain network connectivity has been shown across a swathe of disorders, such as Schizophrenia,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Konrad A, Winterer G | title = Disturbed structural connectivity in schizophrenia primary factor in pathology or epiphenomenon? | journal = Schizophrenia Bulletin | volume = 34 | issue = 1 | pages = 72–92 | date = January 2008 | pmid = 17485733 | doi = 10.1093/schbul/sbm034 | pmc = 2632386 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Skudlarski P, Jagannathan K, Anderson K, Stevens MC, Calhoun VD, Skudlarska BA, Pearlson G | title = Brain connectivity is not only lower but different in schizophrenia: a combined anatomical and functional approach | journal = Biological Psychiatry | volume = 68 | issue = 1 | pages = 61–69 | date = July 2010 | pmid = 20497901 | doi = 10.1016/j.biopsych.2010.03.035 | series = Schizophrenia: N-methyl-D-aspartate Receptor Dysfunction and Cortical Connectivity | pmc = 2900394 }}</ref> Depression,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Long Z, Duan X, Wang Y, Liu F, Zeng L, Zhao JP, Chen H | title = Disrupted structural connectivity network in treatment-naive depression | journal = Progress in Neuro-Psychopharmacology & Biological Psychiatry | volume = 56 | pages = 18–26 | date = January 2015 | pmid = 25092218 | doi = 10.1016/j.pnpbp.2014.07.007 | s2cid = 31447630 }}</ref><ref name = "Wu_2020">{{cite journal | vauthors = Wu F, Tu Z, Sun J, Geng H, Zhou Y, Jiang X, Li H, Kong L | display-authors = 6 | title = Abnormal Functional and Structural Connectivity of Amygdala-Prefrontal Circuit in First-Episode Adolescent Depression: A Combined fMRI and DTI Study | journal = Frontiers in Psychiatry | volume = 10 | page = 983 | date = 2020 | pmid = 32116814 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyt.2019.00983 | pmc = 7013238 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Stroke,<ref name = "Wu_2020" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Park CH, Chang WH, Ohn SH, Kim ST, Bang OY, Pascual-Leone A, Kim YH | title = Longitudinal changes of resting-state functional connectivity during motor recovery after stroke | journal = Stroke | volume = 42 | issue = 5 | pages = 1357–1362 | date = May 2011 | pmid = 21441147 | pmc = 3589816 | doi = 10.1161/STROKEAHA.110.596155 }}</ref> and brain tumor,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bartolomei F, Bosma I, Klein M, Baayen JC, Reijneveld JC, Postma TJ, Heimans JJ, van Dijk BW, de Munck JC, de Jongh A, Cover KS, Stam CJ | display-authors = 6 | title = Disturbed functional connectivity in brain tumour patients: evaluation by graph analysis of synchronization matrices | journal = Clinical Neurophysiology | volume = 117 | issue = 9 | pages = 2039–2049 | date = September 2006 | pmid = 16859985 | doi = 10.1016/j.clinph.2006.05.018 | s2cid = 36779994 }}</ref> underpinning their unique symptoms.

===fMRI with EEG===

Many imaging experts {{Who|date=October 2021}} feel that in order to obtain the best combination of spatial and temporal information from brain activity, both fMRI as well as [[electroencephalography]] (EEG) should be used simultaneously. This dual technique combines the EEG's well documented ability to characterize certain brain states with high temporal resolution and to reveal pathological patterns, with fMRI's (more recently discovered and less well understood) ability to image blood dynamics through the entire brain with high spatial resolution. Up to now, [[EEG-fMRI]] has been mainly seen as an fMRI technique in which the synchronously acquired EEG is used to characterize brain activity ('brain state') across time allowing to map (through statistical parametric mapping, for example) the associated haemodynamic changes.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Majeed W, Magnuson M, Keilholz SD | title = Spatiotemporal dynamics of low frequency fluctuations in BOLD fMRI of the rat | journal = Journal of Magnetic Resonance Imaging | volume = 30 | issue = 2 | pages = 384–393 | date = August 2009 | pmid = 19629982 | pmc = 2758521 | doi = 10.1002/jmri.21848 }}</ref>

The clinical value of these findings is the subject of ongoing investigations, but recent researches suggest an acceptable reliability for EEG-fMRI studies and better sensitivity in higher field scanner. Outside the field of epilepsy, EEG-fMRI has been used to study event-related (triggered by external stimuli) brain responses and provided important new insights into baseline brain activity during resting wakefulness and sleep.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Keilholz SD, Magnuson M, Thompson G | title = Evaluation of data-driven network analysis approaches for functional connectivity MRI | journal = Brain Structure & Function | volume = 215 | issue = 2 | pages = 129–140 | date = August 2010 | pmid = 20853181 | doi = 10.1007/s00429-010-0276-7 | s2cid = 25783833 }}</ref>

===fMRI with TMS===

[[Transcranial magnetic stimulation]] (TMS) uses small and relatively precise magnetic fields to stimulate regions of the cortex without dangerous invasive procedures. When these magnetic fields stimulate an area of the cortex, focal blood flow increases at the site of stimulation as well as at distant sites anatomically connected to the stimulated location. [[Positron emission tomography]] (PET) can then be used to image the brain and changes in blood flow and results show very similar regions of connectivity confirming networks found in fMRI studies and TMS can also be used to support and provide more detailed information on the connected regions.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fox MD, Halko MA, Eldaief MC, Pascual-Leone A | title = Measuring and manipulating brain connectivity with resting state functional connectivity magnetic resonance imaging (fcMRI) and transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 62 | issue = 4 | pages = 2232–2243 | date = October 2012 | pmid = 22465297 | pmc = 3518426 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2012.03.035 }}</ref>

==Potential pitfalls==
{{Further|Functional magnetic resonance imaging#Issues in fMRI}}
Potential pitfalls when using rsfMRI to determine functional network integrity are contamination of the BOLD signal by sources of physiological noise such as heart rate, respiration,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Birn RM, Diamond JB, Smith MA, Bandettini PA | title = Separating respiratory-variation-related fluctuations from neuronal-activity-related fluctuations in fMRI | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 31 | issue = 4 | pages = 1536–1548 | date = July 2006 | pmid = 16632379 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2006.02.048 | s2cid = 3892813 }}</ref><ref name="Chang_2009">{{cite journal | vauthors = Chang C, Glover GH | title = Relationship between respiration, end-tidal CO2, and BOLD signals in resting-state fMRI | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 47 | issue = 4 | pages = 1381–93 | date = October 2009 | pmid = 19393322 | pmc = 2721281 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2009.04.048  }}</ref> and head motion.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Ing A, Schwarzbauer C | title = A dual echo approach to motion correction for functional connectivity studies | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 63 | issue = 3 | pages = 1487–1497 | date = November 2012 | pmid = 22846657 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2012.07.042 | s2cid = 670206 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Van Dijk KR, Sabuncu MR, Buckner RL | title = The influence of head motion on intrinsic functional connectivity MRI | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 59 | issue = 1 | pages = 431–438 | date = January 2012 | pmid = 21810475 | pmc = 3683830 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2011.07.044 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Power JD, Barnes KA, Snyder AZ, Schlaggar BL, Petersen SE | title = Spurious but systematic correlations in functional connectivity MRI networks arise from subject motion | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 59 | issue = 3 | pages = 2142–2154 | date = February 2012 | pmid = 22019881 | pmc = 3254728 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2011.10.018 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Satterthwaite TD, Wolf DH, Loughead J, Ruparel K, Elliott MA, Hakonarson H, Gur RC, Gur RE | display-authors = 6 | title = Impact of in-scanner head motion on multiple measures of functional connectivity: relevance for studies of neurodevelopment in youth | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 60 | issue = 1 | pages = 623–632 | date = March 2012 | pmid = 22233733 | pmc = 3746318 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2011.12.063 }}</ref> These confounding factors can often bias results in studies where patients are compared to healthy controls in the direction of hypothesized effects, for example a lower coherence might be found in the default network in the patient group, while the patient groups also moved more during the scan. Also, it has been shown that the use of global signal regression can produce artificial correlations between a small number of signals (e.g., two or three).<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Saad ZS, Gotts SJ, Murphy K, Chen G, Jo HJ, Martin A, Cox RW | title = Trouble at rest: how correlation patterns and group differences become distorted after global signal regression | journal = Brain Connectivity | volume = 2 | issue = 1 | pages = 25–32 | year = 2012 | pmid = 22432927 | pmc = 3484684 | doi = 10.1089/brain.2012.0080 }}</ref> Fortunately, the brain has many signals.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Cordes D, Nandy RR | title = Estimation of the intrinsic dimensionality of fMRI data | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 29 | issue = 1 | pages = 145–154 | date = January 2006 | pmid = 16202626 | doi = 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2005.07.054 | s2cid = 9228087 }}</ref>

==Current and future applications==

Research using resting state fMRI has the potential to be applied in clinical context, including use in the assessment of many different diseases and [[mental disorder]]s.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Holtbernd F, Eidelberg D | title = Functional brain networks in movement disorders: recent advances | journal = Current Opinion in Neurology | volume = 25 | issue = 4 | pages = 392–401 | date = August 2012 | pmid = 22710361 | pmc = 4554600 | doi = 10.1097/wco.0b013e328355aa94 }}</ref>

===Disease condition and changes in resting state functional connectivity===
* [[Alzheimer's disease]]: decreased connectivity<ref name="Li_2013">{{cite journal | vauthors = Li R, Wu X, Chen K, Fleisher AS, Reiman EM, Yao L | title = Alterations of directional connectivity among resting-state networks in Alzheimer disease | journal = AJNR. American Journal of Neuroradiology | volume = 34 | issue = 2 | pages = 340–5 | date = February 2013 | pmid = 22790250 | pmc = 4097966 | doi = 10.3174/ajnr.A3197 }}</ref>
* [[Mild cognitive impairment]]: abnormal connectivity<ref name = "Liang">{{cite journal | vauthors = Liang P, Wang Z, Yang Y, Li K | title = Three subsystems of the inferior parietal cortex are differently affected in mild cognitive impairment | journal = Journal of Alzheimer's Disease | volume = 30 | issue = 3 | pages = 475–487 | year = 2012 | pmid = 22451310 | doi = 10.3233/JAD-2012-111721 }}</ref>
* [[Autism]]: altered connectivity<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Müller RA, Shih P, Keehn B, Deyoe JR, Leyden KM, Shukla DK | title = Underconnected, but how? A survey of functional connectivity MRI studies in autism spectrum disorders | journal = Cerebral Cortex | volume = 21 | issue = 10 | pages = 2233–2243 | date = October 2011 | pmid = 21378114 | pmc = 3169656 | doi = 10.1093/cercor/bhq296 }}</ref><ref name="Vigneshwaran et al">{{cite journal | vauthors = Subbaraju V, Suresh MB, Sundaram S, Narasimhan S | title = Identifying differences in brain activities and an accurate detection of autism spectrum disorder using resting state functional-magnetic resonance imaging: A spatial filtering approach | journal = Medical Image Analysis | volume = 35 | pages = 375–389 | date = January 2017 | pmid = 27585835 | doi = 10.1016/j.media.2016.08.003 | s2cid = 4922560 }}</ref>
* [[Major depressive disorder|Depression]] and effects of [[antidepressant]] treatment: abnormal connectivity<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Anand A, Li Y, Wang Y, Wu J, Gao S, Bukhari L, Mathews VP, Kalnin A, Lowe MJ | display-authors = 6 | title = Activity and connectivity of brain mood regulating circuit in depression: a functional magnetic resonance study | journal = Biological Psychiatry | volume = 57 | issue = 10 | pages = 1079–1088 | date = May 2005 | pmid = 15866546 | doi = 10.1016/j.biopsych.2005.02.021 | s2cid = 19311022 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Greicius MD, Flores BH, Menon V, Glover GH, Solvason HB, Kenna H, Reiss AL, Schatzberg AF | display-authors = 6 | title = Resting-state functional connectivity in major depression: abnormally increased contributions from subgenual cingulate cortex and thalamus | journal = Biological Psychiatry | volume = 62 | issue = 5 | pages = 429–437 | date = September 2007 | pmid = 17210143 | pmc = 2001244 | doi = 10.1016/j.biopsych.2006.09.020 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Anand A, Li Y, Wang Y, Wu J, Gao S, Bukhari L, Mathews VP, Kalnin A, Lowe MJ | display-authors = 6 | title = Antidepressant effect on connectivity of the mood-regulating circuit: an FMRI study | language = En | journal = Neuropsychopharmacology | volume = 30 | issue = 7 | pages = 1334–1344 | date = July 2005 | pmid = 15856081 | doi = 10.1038/sj.npp.1300725 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Anand A, Li Y, Wang Y, Gardner K, Lowe MJ | title = Reciprocal effects of antidepressant treatment on activity and connectivity of the mood regulating circuit: an FMRI study | journal = The Journal of Neuropsychiatry and Clinical Neurosciences | volume = 19 | issue = 3 | pages = 274–282 | date = 2007-07-01 | pmid = 17827412 | pmc = 3465666 | doi = 10.1176/jnp.2007.19.3.274 }}</ref>
* [[Bipolar disorder]] and effects of [[mood stabilizers]]: abnormal connectivity and network properties<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Anand A, Li Y, Wang Y, Lowe MJ, Dzemidzic M | title = Resting state corticolimbic connectivity abnormalities in unmedicated bipolar disorder and unipolar depression | journal = Psychiatry Research | volume = 171 | issue = 3 | pages = 189–198 | date = March 2009 | pmid = 19230623 | pmc = 3001251 | doi = 10.1016/j.pscychresns.2008.03.012 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Spielberg JM, Beall EB, Hulvershorn LA, Altinay M, Karne H, Anand A | title = Resting State Brain Network Disturbances Related to Hypomania and Depression in Medication-Free Bipolar Disorder | language = En | journal = Neuropsychopharmacology | volume = 41 | issue = 13 | pages = 3016–3024 | date = December 2016 | pmid = 27356764 | pmc = 5101549 | doi = 10.1038/npp.2016.112 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Altinay MI, Hulvershorn LA, Karne H, Beall EB, Anand A | title = Differential Resting-State Functional Connectivity of Striatal Subregions in Bipolar Depression and Hypomania | journal = Brain Connectivity | volume = 6 | issue = 3 | pages = 255–265 | date = April 2016 | pmid = 26824737 | pmc = 4827275 | doi = 10.1089/brain.2015.0396 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Altinay M, Karne H, Anand A | title = Lithium monotherapy associated clinical improvement effects on amygdala-ventromedial prefrontal cortex resting state connectivity in bipolar disorder | journal = Journal of Affective Disorders | volume = 225 | pages = 4–12 | date = January 2018 | pmid = 28772145 | pmc = 5844774 | doi = 10.1016/j.jad.2017.06.047 }}</ref>
* [[Schizophrenia]]: disrupted networks<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Venkataraman A, Whitford TJ, Westin CF, [[Polina Golland|Golland P]], Kubicki M | title = Whole brain resting state functional connectivity abnormalities in schizophrenia | journal = Schizophrenia Research | volume = 139 | issue = 1–3 | pages = 7–12 | date = August 2012 | pmid = 22633528 | pmc = 3393792 | doi = 10.1016/j.schres.2012.04.021 | hdl = 1721.1/100215 }}</ref>
* [[Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder]] (ADHD): altered "small networks" and thalamus changes<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Uddin LQ, Kelly AM, Biswal BB, Margulies DS, Shehzad Z, Shaw D, Ghaffari M, Rotrosen J, Adler LA, Castellanos FX, Milham MP | display-authors = 6 | title = Network homogeneity reveals decreased integrity of default-mode network in ADHD | journal = Journal of Neuroscience Methods | volume = 169 | issue = 1 | pages = 249–254 | date = March 2008 | pmid = 18190970 | doi = 10.1016/j.jneumeth.2007.11.031 | s2cid = 35668659 | author1-link = Lucina Uddin }}</ref>
* [[Aging brain]]: disruption of brain systems and motor network<ref name="Li_2013" />
* [[Epilepsy]]: disruption and decrease/increase in connectivity<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Zang YF, Zhao SG | title = Resting-state fMRI studies in epilepsy | journal = Neuroscience Bulletin | volume = 28 | issue = 4 | pages = 449–455 | date = August 2012 | pmid = 22833042 | pmc = 5561896 | doi = 10.1007/s12264-012-1255-1 }}</ref>
* [[Parkinson's disease]]: altered connectivity<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Tessitore A, Amboni M, Esposito F, Russo A, Picillo M, Marcuccio L, Pellecchia MT, Vitale C, Cirillo M, Tedeschi G, Barone P | display-authors = 6 | title = Resting-state brain connectivity in patients with Parkinson's disease and freezing of gait | journal = Parkinsonism & Related Disorders | volume = 18 | issue = 6 | pages = 781–787 | date = July 2012 | pmid = 22510204 | doi = 10.1016/j.parkreldis.2012.03.018 }}</ref>
* [[Obsessive compulsive disorder]]: increase/decrease in connectivity<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Li P, Li S, Dong Z, Luo J, Han H, Xiong H, Guo Z, Li Z | display-authors = 6 | title = Altered resting state functional connectivity patterns of the anterior prefrontal cortex in obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = NeuroReport | volume = 23 | issue = 11 | pages = 681–686 | date = August 2012 | pmid = 22692554 | doi = 10.1097/wnr.0b013e328355a5fe | s2cid = 41049732 }}</ref>
* [[Pain disorder]]: altered connectivity<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Otti A, Guendel H, Wohlschläger A, Zimmer C, Noll-Hussong M | title = Frequency shifts in the anterior default mode network and the salience network in chronic pain disorder | journal = BMC Psychiatry | volume = 13 | page = 84 | date = March 2013 | pmid = 23497482 | pmc = 3616999 | doi = 10.1186/1471-244x-13-84 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Otti A, Guendel H, Henningsen P, Zimmer C, Wohlschlaeger AM, Noll-Hussong M | title = Functional network connectivity of pain-related resting state networks in somatoform pain disorder: an exploratory fMRI study | journal = Journal of Psychiatry & Neuroscience | volume = 38 | issue = 1 | pages = 57–65 | date = January 2013 | pmid = 22894821 | pmc = 3529220 | doi = 10.1503/jpn.110187 }}</ref>
* [[Anorexia nervosa]]: connectivity alterations within corticolimbic circuitry and of [[insular cortex]]<ref name="Gaudio2016">{{cite journal | vauthors = Gaudio S, Wiemerslage L, Brooks SJ, Schiöth HB | title = A systematic review of resting-state functional-MRI studies in anorexia nervosa: Evidence for functional connectivity impairment in cognitive control and visuospatial and body-signal integration | journal = Neuroscience and Biobehavioral Reviews | volume = 71 | pages = 578–589 | date = December 2016 | pmid = 27725172 | doi = 10.1016/j.neubiorev.2016.09.032 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Other types of current and future clinical applications for resting state fMRI include identifying group differences in brain disease, obtaining diagnostic and prognostic information, longitudinal studies and treatment effects, clustering in heterogeneous disease states, and pre-operative mapping and targeting intervention.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fox MD, Greicius M | title = Clinical applications of resting state functional connectivity | journal = Frontiers in Systems Neuroscience | volume = 4 | page = 19 | year = 2010 | pmid = 20592951 | pmc = 2893721 | doi = 10.3389/fnsys.2010.00019 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Due to its lack of reliance on task performance and cognitive demands, resting state fMRI can be a useful tool in assessing brain alterations in disorders of impaired consciousness and cognition, as well as paediatric populations.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lee |first1=MH |last2=Smyser |first2=CD |last3=Shimony |first3=JS |title=Resting-state fMRI: a review of methods and clinical applications. |journal=AJNR. American Journal of Neuroradiology |date=October 2013 |volume=34 |issue=10 |pages=1866–72 |doi=10.3174/ajnr.A3263 |pmid=22936095|pmc=4035703 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
